sentences.text: During, actually, after [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the priest has his document and everything was arranged, next Sunday, naturally, like everybody else, was going to the church and I was also going with the people to the church.
And the church, I knew exactly what to do because when I was a little boy, I had a nanny.
And she was-- every Sunday she was going to a Mass, the Catholic church.
And I was going with her for a walk.
And she-- I was-- she was taking me to the church.
Naturally, my parents didn't knew about it.
Didn't know about it.
My father wouldn't allow anything like this, for me to go to the church.
No, once we went over for a walk on Sunday, and she went to the church and asked if I wanted.
I wouldn't, you know, I was a little boy.
I would say, why not?
I would go any place because I didn't know how the church would look when I was-- and when I walk in from the first day was really impressed me.

Especially impressed me, those altar boys impressed me in the white robes, and they been marching procession with the big-- with the crosses, with the figures, with and extinguishing the candles, and lighting the candles, and moving and helping the priest.
And I said, this a nice job.
And I also envied them.
And I also was thinking gee, I wish I would be an altar boy when I grow up.
Very, very small.
Oh, maybe five or so.
And, and since then, every Sunday I want to go to the church.
Naturally, my father didn't ask, and I didn't tell.
And what's normal for a walk, going to be stop in the church.
So I knew when to kneel and how to behave.
And I knew the prayers even by heart.
I was so little boy in-- I was small but I knew.
And so when I, when I came to a church, I felt quite comfortable.
Not like-- it's not like I have to look at what everybody what is doing or when to kneel.

Naturally, I knew-- I didn't know everything and didn't know all the prayers, but I realized the moment people started moving that I'd have to kneel and, or pray, I'd mumble or-- And on the first Sunday he was from the pulpit, he was-- had a sermon.
And during the sermon he said, we have one newcomer to our parish, parish, who did more to the parish than all of you sinners.
And with a hand, he--
--pointed, pointed to me.
But all of you sinners, pointed to them.
And I was sitting way in the back.
And all the eyes was turned.
Turned my side.
And I wish at that times that I would bury myself in the ground, but wasn't-- but I held my head straight and didn't make any gesture.
Just sit down and serious.
And after the sermon, everybody was, after the service, everybody was coming to me and asking me what-- and everybody was inviting, inviting me.
And every house since then was open to me.
So everything was falling in the right place.
People asked me if I need food, if I need this, if I need that.
They were just-- just wanted because I did so much with the parish they all have to do something for me also.
And they choose they didn't ask if I am Jew, and I didn't tell them that I am Jew.
And they didn't think about this is a Jew.
Would go to German authorities and, and behave like that at their church also.
The only thing what, really, what I was afraid of that-- every Jew was circumcised.
So when I was in the field, over there in the village, you don't go around to the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
Toilet.
The outhouse like we call it in the village, but right on the field.
So when I was with the cows, I was right-- but I was always standing so nobody would see me.
Like some other people were so, they were all standing.
And the men asked me, what, what do you have to be afraid of?
And I said, I'm shy.
I, I'm not used to it.
They knew that I am city boy.
And they called me engineer since then because I-- my behavior was a little bit different than theirs.
You know, this was mostly honest people, act different, talk different, behave different, and especially this was in a small village, eastern village.
And the peasants, mostly were peasants, and they act different.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And also, they had-- whatever they needed or a question, in every field, they came to me.
Obviously, I was like a doctor, like a minister, like a lawyer, like an engineer.
They called me engineer.
Or they like call by name, but the little engineer, the city engineer.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Usually, they didn't have whiskey.
During this time, whiskey wasn't available.
But so they, but they have to have some whiskey so they all made moonshine from grain and from potatoes, which was also illegal.
It was also made in the woods.
So they'd been inviting me out.
So the first time, I had to-- there wasn't any hydrometers at that time.
You tasted.
If it was still strong or-- So they asked me to do it.
And I have to try every time, every few minutes if it's still strong.
And finally when it was, was weaker, I said weaker.
So they used different bottles.
After I said stop, it's water coming out.
I was the expert.
The only thing, I was drinking, like a young man, you know, wine, through the kiddish at home, or sometimes whiskey socially.
And I have to-- usually didn't drink much.
But I had to show that I can drink better than, more than they.
So I drink, and I didn't-- but after any, every session like that, I was really-- Drunk.
--Light in the head.
But I cannot, I have to watch what I am saying, when-- and after that, I was always coming with a horse and wagon.
But was always was in the woods far away.
So they would put me on the wagon, gave me those, the whip, and whip the horse.
And the horse ran-- Back home.
And the horse knew better the roads than I.
Don't matter what time it was.
It was snowing or during summertime, the horse always knew, and it was going fast home.
And I [? didn't call ?] for them.
So I was coming-- when I came home, they were still unload me from the wagon, put me onto the sack.
And they knew that I'm invited to-- but usually you wouldn't say you've been making moonshine.
They also kill a pig because also it was illegal kill a-- kill a pig because everything was registered with a Lithuanian or German authorities.
And if you wanted to kill an animal, you have to have a permit for your own use.
But if they wanted little more, all the pigs were registered.
But they always saved some pigs which were not registered.
So at the same time, they killed a pig.
So they gave me the honor, the [INAUDIBLE] to kill the pig.
They've been holding the pig, tied up the pig.
Pig was squealing.
And I suppose to pierce the big, long knife right in the heart.
This was--
I couldn't do it.
I told him I don't know.
I would ruin the whole thing.
I don't know.
I never in my life kill a pig, never kill an animal.
And I may ruin-- so they, so they killed, but I have to at least have the-- was a pen to save the blood because they made sausages from the blood.
And on this, I couldn't say no.
So when they, when they kill the pig, the blood start piercing, they put my hands so as I don't know where to keep.
And I hold, held the, the pen.
And the blood was running in the pen.
And I look at it.
I couldn't look at other way.
And how could I do it?
I don't know how I could do it.
And do it in like, like that.
But this was everything.
I grew in into the village, and they respected me.
And this was going for quite a while.
One day, I was walking in the, in the area over there.
Four men came, keeping straight.
And I knew right away was from the city because you could recognize a city man.
And they pointed in my direction because he was asking by my, by the name.
And he came to me and asked me if I am so-and-so.
I said yes.
I would like to talk to you.
So I said, OK, let's talk.
But the first thing to my mind came is that he knows that I am a Jew, and he wants blackmail me.
But I knew I don't have nothing to risk, only life because usually if they blackmail, they want some gold or money or some valuables that you have.
I didn't have anything, so I couldn't offer him anything.
So I was thinking-- They can't win your life.
There's no other way.
So let's talk.
So he said, no, we cannot talk here.
We have to go inside.
So at that time, when he said inside, immediately, I said, oh, this must be it.
But we came in.
We sat down on a, was a rough, wooden bench.
We sat down, and he said he knows that I am an officer of the Polish army.
And he is also officer of underground army.
And he came, he inducted me in the--
Underground army.
First of all I was playing hard to get.
And I said, I never was in the army.
I never had any army experience.
I'm too young to serve in the army.
I couldn't be an officer.
But whatever he said, I knew that he know only from one man because I was stating those things only to one person.
And this was the organist in the church.
He was a patriot.
He was also telling about his experiences.
And we talk about he was anti-Communist and anti-, anti-Lithuanian, and anti-German.
And then we talk about things like that.
So immediately, I knew that I'm on the right track.
But I still play hard to get.
And, and I told him after the discussion, I said, you came here.
I don't you.
You tell me that, you are telling me that you know about, all about me.
You must have heard from someplace.
I don't know where.
But I-- you don't have any credentials that you are-- Telling the truth?
Officer.
I said, maybe you are a spy.
Maybe you are a German spy, and you want to know from me why I was serving here.
So he says, so he said, he took the hand to the back pocket and took out a gun.
And he said those are my credential.
So I thought very hard.
Under those circumstances, I don't have a gun.
I don't have any weapon.
So I have to tell what-- we have to agree with everything what you said.
I don't have any other choice.
If I serve in the army or I didn't serve the army, if any, you would say that I was Communist.
I have to admit that I am Communist because I don't have a gun.
So he said, oh, you played the right cards.
We need people like that.
And took the gun and put on the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] table-- was wooden table, I saw-- and went to the wall and took out a crucifix.
There was never a room was a crucifix.
I said never a room.
It wasn't an apartment.
It was a hut-- you know, straw-covered, like a log cabin.
And the doors were very un-- the windows were very primitive.
And he took my hand, put on the crucifix, and said repeat after me.
And he started with the prayer in the name of Father and Son, Holy Ghost, Ghost.
Not sure, I knew I repeated it.
I tried to even before he was saying I was reciting because by that time I already remembered well the prayers.
And he said I am being inducted in the underground.
And I will behave in the way officers should behave.
In case I will reveal any secrets to anybody, to German military or, under any circumstance, if I would be punished, I would be beaten ever, I would be, and I would be alive.
I would be sentenced without court-martial to death.
If anything, give out to any civilian, any friend, I couldn't reveal any secrets or share any information with anybody, everything under penalty of death.
Yeah, but I have to play-- I said, here I'm looking for underground.
I know where to go, where to-- and here comes on the plate to me.
But I play hard to get purposely to show that I don't trust him.
I don't know who he is.
And, but inside, I felt like in heaven.
I said, this is really what I was looking forward.
And instead looking forward, it came right to my doors.
And he told me for the orders, I have to come to the city.
He gave me the address.
Nothing could be written.
Everything has to be memorized.
I have to repeat it to him what he told me.
And I have to-- he said to memorize.
Well, and at that time, the memory was still good.
And on such, such, such day, in such, such place, he told me it will be a store.
He explained me what kind of store it will be.
And it'll be a man.
And if somebody will be in the store besides the man, to wait until the-- the business-- they will finish the business.
And he gave me-- and to ask for a souvenir box because the store was only with a junk, with the use.
Things like, during the war, everything was for sale.
You know, new things was hard to get.
Pardon me?
I can't hear.
Pardon me.
Because this was a set-up store with the little schmuckness, you know, little things for a kitchen, for, for-- But he asked me to ask for a box, square souvenir box.
I suppose to ask the man.
And the man supposed to tell me, no, he doesn't have a square.
So I was supposed to ask him if he had a oval one.
So he's supposed to tell me, no, he doesn't have one.
So I suppose to ask him what shape he has.
And he's supposed to tell me a round one.
I said the round one will do it.
And this was the--
And, and later he's supposed to ask me, what is my name.
So he only, also know my real name, the only, the name which I selected, right, my pet name, which you are to be called in the army.
In Polish, it's szczupak, which means a pike.
Pike because it, pike eats other fish.
And we had-- and, and, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and after, I, I suppose to ask him what is that name?
And he told me also what other fellow been there.
And after this exchange, he would-- the guy felt that-- I couldn't deviate from the conversation.
The conversation has to be exactly.
I have to memorize.
I even remember now.
I don't think I don't remember the fellow's name.
But I remember on which street it was.
I don't remember the name, but I see the store.
And I came.
And he said on certain Saturday to come at such, such hour.
So I walked.
I didn't-- because I couldn't tell the farmers that I need-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And after I was inducted, also he told me that I cannot work in the-- on a farm--
--Because the Germans, or many people capable to work, naturally physically, building roads and they inducted in the, their engineering units which were called TODD, T-O-D-D.
It doesn't mean death, but then this is after the name of the general who was in charge of those units like SS units or as other troop [? not-- ?] this was called Todd [?
Troopen. ?] And they inducted civilian to those units.
And because I was in these territories--
I would have to be.
So to be protected, they gave me-- was also in the same village, way on the hill, was a big [INAUDIBLE], big farm, and this belonged before the war to a Polish general.
But after, when the Communists came, they nationalized naturally.
And when the Germans came, they put a administrator, administrator on that.
And I get that.
And I had the job over that.
I was put in charge of fish hatcheries because they had ponds, man-made ponds, square ponds where they raised fish.
And the fish were supposed to go to German army.
We couldn't-- not-- we-- they couldn't sell to any civilian store or to any civilian people.
All they-- because this was a national property, this is a German property.
All the fish had to go.
We could have for our own use, but all of the other would be delivered to a depot to a General.
But unfortunate, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we never delivered any fish.
Always either was some, some disease or some the ponds some they're on their way, some ponds, then broke.
Always we found some excuse that we couldn't deliver the fish.
And I was put in charge of the fish.
I didn't know nothing about the fish.
The only I knew about the fish is Friday evening to dinner where we were always fish.
Pardon me?
Gefilte fish or carp or or pike or-- for gefilte fish, it was pike.
And you know, I remember also the woman who work at home.
And it was the housekeeper.
How she killed the fish that I couldn't look.
I don't-- that's the only thing I don't know about the fish.
But they gave me some German books, and in one week I became an expert-- 'expert' in the quotation mark.
And the reason why because I received [NON-ENGLISH].
This is one word.
This means that I am indispensable in civilian life so I couldn't be taken to any German units or to serve in any other capacity or are to be sent to German, to Germany for work.
I have to be right here because I am indispensable.
And they want me over there.
And the manager, he was a Pole and he know why, he was, he belonged also to underground to the Krajowa.
No.
How could they-- if anybody-- Jewish?
If anybody--
Pardon me?
If anybody-- [INTERPOSING VOICES] If I am a Jew?
First of all, nobody would want to keep me in the house.
Second of all, people would squeal that I am Jew.
And many, many Jews lost their life because when the Poles or Lithuanian found out that that is a Jew, immediately they squeal to the German, the Germans.
And they-- no, it was no excuse.

Even if it was, as you say, let, let down the pens and no, either arrest them or shoot, or shot, and therefore the women could more pretend that if they have occasion, yeah, facility, and the conditions are right.
But with the men, there was no excuse, therefore, I had to be very careful.
Later.
In the beginning, I didn't know.
But I knew what happened in the Jews because like when they had been going to Paneriai, which was-- they been shooting the Jews.
This was still I was in, when I was coming to Vilna, I knew what they were telling me.
They'd been taking-- they'd take them from ghettos.
Even before the ghettos, they'd been taking Jews to Paneriai and later from-- which was ditches, they had been laid in the ditch and shot, a known fact.
And I still remember, this was always early in the morning, they'd been walking.
You can see the walk and you knew that this is Jews on way to-- and this was not, not Germans, maybe one, two, three Germans with all Lithuanians in uniforms.
Pardon me?
I was still in Vilna.
Vilna.
And, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and this was always early in the morning not during the day, early in the morning.
This was on [?
Mitskevitcha ?] Street where I was living.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] so, you know, there are many women, young women, and here is man from the city comes in, they want to make Anschluss, and I have to be very careful.
I was always, no, I don't want to have anything to do with women.
Many women also later when some young women had been coming to the farm to his family also a young man is here, might as well.
I always stand at a distance because I couldn't afford to reveal that I am a Jew.
Pardon me?
Yeah, very many women because I was a city boy not a farm boy.
And also to the one younger woman at the young farmer and helped me.

And therefore, there was one doctor over there which I became a very close friend because the only intelligencia out there was myself, a doctor, and a federal administrator of the-- with his family, and forester also, very intelligent man because he was studying forestry.
And we're like click.
We are together.
But was one doctor which I was coming to his house, and he has a library.
And I naturally always go and look in the books.
But I was looking in the books because I heard that there are occasions where a man either has a some disease or some infection and has to be circumcised.
I didn't call it circumcising, I call it surgery.
And I-- so I look at the books and study and I studied effects under what circumstances, and in my mind I established a story.
And you could-- you could wake me up during the night or under any conditions, and I will know the story.
You would ask me to repeat 100 times.
I knew exactly is that I had such infection and I have so many medication.
Nothing helped.
So I was operated in such and such hospital by such, such doctor, at such and such time, and there were so many days in the hospital.
And I know the story forwards and backwards.
I made up the story.
So if you were to wake me up during the night or I was drunk or anything, I knew the story by heart.
And but this wouldn't help, but at least for myself, I was thinking some excuse.
But I didn't have to use any.
It was also in the-- or in the village they had that like a spa.
What is a spa?
A hot with the stones and they put fire under it and they threw water on it, you know, and the steam.
And so it's always Saturday before Sunday so I would-- was clean.
The whole village was coming.
I wasn't coming.
Like sauna, yeah.
And they ask me why-- but primitive sauna, just-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Why are you not coming?
I said, there's lice over there.
I'm afraid to catch-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
No germs.
The lice, which--
Lice, excuse me.
Lice.
And they knew that I am city boy so I am the same thing as they have been.
One pond was what we were allowed to bathe-- our people and people who couldn't have bath tubs.
And everyone was bathing who can-- who could bathe, and I wasn't bathing.
Why?
Because, you know, we wouldn't bathing with a bathing suit.
We were bathing without any clothes.
So I said, not, not that I'm shy, but it was so much algae.
And I said when I jump in the pond, I will come out more dirty than I will wash in it.
Algae has-- very much against it.
And the algae flowers, and it flowers by the green leaves.
And I couldn't bathe in it.
And on the clean ponds, it wasn't allowed, what with fish were over there.
And also, the first time I saw how was they feed the fish.
They put a big stick.
On the big stick was a little plot that-- a little board.
And on the board was some old deceased meat.
And the worms were in the meat.
And the worms were falling in the water.
And this was the fish were-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] did you cut off were?
